content:
3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination Hao Huang , Yanji Tian , Diaa AbuKhadra

, Nitzan Maman , Roy Bernstein *

Zuckerberg Institute for Water Research, Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben–Gurion University of the Negev, Sede–Boqer Campus, 84990, Israel

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Effluent desalinationReverse osmosis pretreatmentPolyzwitterionicPolymer brushUltrafiltrationAntifouling surface

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: A B S T R A C T Ultrafiltration (UF) membranes are promising options for reverse osmosis (RO) desalination pretreatment, but membrane fouling limits their broad application. This study presents a three-dimensional (3D) poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted polyethersulfone UF membrane with excellent antifouling properties during the filtration of secondary wastewater effluent, providing an effective pretreatment to reduce fouling during RO desalination. Membrane characterization confirmed successful grafting and also demonstrated that extended polymerization (2–6 h) increased surface hydrophilicity, reduced charge and roughness, and improved antifouling performance. Filtration of secondary wastewater effluent for 5 days at 95 % water recovery using the modified membrane resulted in only 24 % increase in transmembrane pressure (TMP). Surface characterization and quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation (QCM-D) measurements indicated enhanced antifouling properties owing to low bacterial deposition and reduced organic matter adsorption. Effluent treated with the modified membrane was of superior quality compared to that treated with conventional tertiary treatment, and using modified membrane-treated effluent as a feed for RO desalination led to better performance than the conventionally treated effluent, as demonstrated by the stable TMP over 12 h desalination.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: feed for RO desalination led to better performance than the conventionally treated effluent, as demonstrated by the stable TMP over 12 h desalination. These findings highlight the potential of grafted UF membranes to minimize fouling and increase RO efficiency, thus helping to produce potable water in water-scarce regions.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: 1. Introduction Wastewater reuse is a promising solution to alleviate global water scarcity, particularly in arid and semi-arid regions [1,2]. Reverse osmosis (RO) is the leading technology for producing high-quality water for direct or indirect potable use from wastewater effluent [3,4]. In current practice, RO is typically fed with conventionally treated tertiary effluent—coagulation followed by flocculation, granular filtra­tion, and disinfection [5]. Yet these unit processes deliver feedwater whose quality fluctuates widely, compromising RO performance and long-term membrane integrity [6–9]. Ultrafiltration (UF) membranes have emerged as a viable alternative as a more robust pretreatment step. By reliably removing suspended solids, colloids, and microorganisms across a range of raw-water qualities, UF can stabilize RO feed charac­teristics and enhance overall desalination efficiency [6,10–12]. How­ever, a major challenge is membrane fouling, which reduces performance, increases operational costs, and shortens the membrane’s lifespan [13,14]. Developing fouling-resistant membranes is a funda­mental strategy for alleviating these challenges. The modification of membranes with antifouling polymers has been extensively studied to combat fouling [15,16]. Zwitterionic polymers, primarily poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) (poly(SBMA)), have attracted attention because of their promising antifouling properties, especially when applied in a polymer brush [17].

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: thacrylate) (poly(SBMA)), have attracted attention because of their promising antifouling properties, especially when applied in a polymer brush [17]. The brush structure improves antifouling performance compared with non-brush polymer coatings by reducing foulant adhesion [18–20]. Several studies have successfully grafted linear poly(SBMA) brushes from the surface of UF membranes, achieving excellent antifouling properties during the filtration of various model foulants [21–23]. Further improvement of membrane properties, including improved antifouling performance, has been reported for grafted polymer brushes with nonlinear structures [24]. Our recent work showed that branched poly(SBMA) brushes outperformed their linear counterparts in terms of membrane fouling resistance during the filtration of complex solutions, an effect ascribed to the greater steric

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: Available online 8 August 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync-nd/4.0/ ). hindrance of the branched brushes [25]. Extending this concept, 3D bottlebrushes could provide even broader surface coverage, even at lower graft densities; their laterally overlapping side chains form a dense steric barrier that blocks foulants intrusion [26]. To date, however, most bottlebrush-modified membranes have relied on “grafting-to” strategies, the polymers become chemically linked to the surface and form the brush, which cannot generate the high-density coverage required for optimal antifouling performance [20,27]. Employing a “grafting-from” approach, we recently produced a 3D poly(SBMA) brush (pseudo-bot­tlebrushes) modified RO polyamide membrane that shows markedly superior anti-scaling performance in brackish-water desalination, highlighting the advantages of bottlebrush structures in membrane fouling control [28]. In this research, we developed a poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush structure grafted polyethersulfone (PES) UF membrane, which resul­ted in significant antifouling performance during the tertiary treatment of secondary wastewater effluent prior to RO desalination.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: brane, which resul­ted in significant antifouling performance during the tertiary treatment of secondary wastewater effluent prior to RO desalination. A layer of the pseudo-bottlebrushes was grafted from the PES membrane surface by the free radical polymerization of hydroxymethyl methacrylate (HEMA), followed by activators generated by electron transfer (AGET) atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP). The poly(HEMA) modifi­cation provided anchoring points for attaching the ATRP initiator, which facilitated the growth of sulfobetaine methacrylate (SBMA) chains orthogonal to the HEMA chains. The membranes were charac­terized to confirm the grafting, and the effect of poly(SBMA) grafting duration on the membrane’s surface and cross-section properties and on antifouling performance against bovine serum albumin (BSA) were investigated. Based on these findings, the optimal grafted membrane was then tested in the pretreatment of real secondary wastewater effluent for 24 h and then for 5 days at a high, realistic water recovery ratio of 95 %. We then related foulant–membrane interactions to the change of interfacial characteristics using quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation (QCM-D) measurements, thereby providing insights into the organic antifouling mechanisms achieved by poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush grafting.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ssipation (QCM-D) measurements, thereby providing insights into the organic antifouling mechanisms achieved by poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush grafting. Lastly, RO desalination experiments compared effluent pretreated using the optimal grafted UF membrane and real tertiary treated effluent (coagulation, sand filtration, and chlorine disinfection). Effluent quality and the fouling layer were analyzed to explain the effectiveness of the pretreatment using the optimal grafted UF membrane to improve RO desalination performance. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials HEMA, 2-Bromobutyryl bromide (BiBB), SBMA, polyethylene glycols (PEGs) (Mn = 35, 100, 200 kDa), 2,2′-bipyridine (bpy), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). CuBr2 was from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium). Sodium acetate and Lascorbic acid (L-AscOH) were from Carlo Erba Reagents (Cornaredo, Italy). Absolute methanol (analytical reagent grade; AR), glutaraldehyde (50 %) (AR), acetonitrile (AR), diethyl ether (AR), glycerol (AR), eth­ylenediaminetetraacetic acid (AR), sodium hydroxide (chemically pure), and hydrochloric acid (32 %) were from BioLab (Jerusalem, Israel). All chemicals were used without further purification. All ex­periments used deionized (DI) water from a water purification system (Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA). 2.2.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: used without further purification. All ex­periments used deionized (DI) water from a water purification system (Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA). 2.2. Membrane modification Poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrushes were grafted from the surface of a PES UF membrane (200 kDa, Synder Filtration, Vacaville, CA, USA) via free radical polymerization followed by AGET-ATRP, as described in our previous studies [25,28,29]. Briefly, poly(HEMA) was first grafted onto the PES membrane via free-radical UV-induced grafting polymerization, and the resulting membrane was labeled the PES-OH membrane.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: Subsequently, the ATRP initiator (BiBB) was attached to the PES-OH membrane via esterification to create a PES–OH–Br membrane. Further grafting by AGET-surface initiated (SI)-ATRP of poly(SBMA) brushes from the surface of the PES–OH–Br membrane yielded poly (SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted PES UF membranes labeled as Mx, where x indicates the ATRP polymerization time in hours. Scheme 1shows the grafting steps (see supplementary information Text S1 for further details). 2.3. Membrane characterization The membranes’ surface and cross-sectional properties and separa­tion performance were characterized. The surface properties were characterized by using attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), water contact angle (CAw) measurements, zeta-potential mea­surements, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and scanning electron mi­croscopy (SEM). High-resolution cross-sectional SEM images were obtained by focused ion beam (FIB)-SEM, following our previous study [30]. For FIB-SEM, a thin platinum layer (~8 nm) was initially sputtered onto the PES membranes prior to their modification to differentiate the grafted layer from the PES substrate clearly. Separation performance was assessed by considering water permeability, salt rejection, and molecular weight cut-off (MWCO). The supplementary information gives details of the characterization (Texts S2 and S3). 2.4. Fouling experiments 2.4.1.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: olecular weight cut-off (MWCO). The supplementary information gives details of the characterization (Texts S2 and S3). 2.4. Fouling experiments 2.4.1. Bovine serum albumin filtration experiments BSA fouling experiments (n = 2) were performed in dead-end filtration mode under constant flux conditions and with stirring at 500 rpm. A peristaltic pump drove the feed solution from a 2 L reservoir into a stainless-steel filtration cell with an effective area of 12.56 cm2. The cell and reservoir were filled with BSA solution (100 ppm BSA in 8.5 mM NaCl and 1.5 mM CaCl2) and kept at 25 ± 1 ◦C using a heating plate (Fig. S1). Each filtration experiment comprised six of the following cy­cles: filtration for 59 min at 35 LMH, followed by 1 min of backwash at 115 LMH (adjusted by changing the pump frequency). This resulted in around 95 % water recovery ratio. Constant flux was verified by using an electronic balance connected to a computer, and fouling was assessed by online monitoring of changes in transmembrane pressure (TMP) using a pressure transducer connected to a datalogger (FieldLogger, NOVUS, Canoas, Brazil). 2.4.2. Real secondary wastewater effluent filtration experiments Fouling experiment with real secondary effluent (collected from a wastewater treatment plant in Yeruham, Israel) was tested similarly to the BSA fouling experiments, but for 24 h and with a feed and bleed mode implemented to maintain a fresh feed solution (n = 2).

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: as tested similarly to the BSA fouling experiments, but for 24 h and with a feed and bleed mode implemented to maintain a fresh feed solution (n = 2). The feed and bleed mode involved pumping feed stored at around 4 ◦C into the 2 L reservoir using a peristaltic pump while excess effluent was removed by overflow. The hydraulic retention time was 12 h. The permeate from the poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted PES UF membrane with the lowest fouling was collected for subsequent RO desalination experi­ments. To evaluate long-term antifouling performance, additional 5-day filtration experiments using real secondary wastewater effluent were conducted. 2.5. QCM-D measurements 2.5.1. Preparation of QCM-D sensors Gold QCM-D sensors (QSX 301, Qsense, Gothenburg, Sweden) were treated with UV/O3 (BioFORCE Nanosciences, Ames, IA, USA) for 10 min, followed by soaking in 2 % sodium dodecyl sulfate for 30 min, washing thoroughly with double-distilled water (DDW), and drying using N2. A PES-coated QCM-D sensor was prepared by spin coating

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: (WS-400BZ-6NPP, Laurell, San Franciso, CA, USA) 80 μL 0.5 % w/v PES (Ultrason E 6020P, BASF, Ludwigshafen, Germany) in dichloromethane on a QCM-D sensor at 4000 rpm for 30 s. A poly(SBMA) pseudobottlebrush-coated QCM-D sensor was prepared following the same protocol as for the M6 membrane (Text S1). The modified QCM-D sen­sors were analyzed using CAw measurements, XPS, and ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (with a variable-angle, single-reflection ATR accessory; Text S2) to verify the presence of PES and poly(SBMA). 2.5.2. QCM-D adsorption experiment Adherence and adsorption kinetics (n = 2) were assessed using real effluent organic matter (EfOM) obtained by filtering secondary effluent with a 0.45 μm polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) filter (Millipore). The experiments were conducted using QCM-D (E4 QSense Analyzer, Biolin Scientific, Gothenburg, Sweden) in five sequential steps: DDW for 20 min; background solution (BS) for 30 min; EfOM for 120 min; BS for 40 min; and DDW for 40 min. Solutions were injected at a 100 μL/min flow rate and a fixed temperature (23 ◦C). The BS was prepared by mimicking the ionic composition of the real secondary wastewater effluent. Realtime changes in frequency and dissipation for the original, PES-coated, and poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-coated sensors were monitored at different overtones. 2.6.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: n frequency and dissipation for the original, PES-coated, and poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-coated sensors were monitored at different overtones. 2.6. RO desalination experiments RO desalination experiments (n = 2) were conducted using a laboratory-scale cross-flow filtration system (Inspector Colossus, Convergence, Amsterdam, Netherlands) connected to a cross-flow filtration membrane cell (CF042, Sterlitech, Auburn, WA, USA). The cell’s flow channel was 0.23 cm deep, 3.9 cm wide, and had an effective area of 42 cm2. The experiments were performed at a constant permeate flux of 30 LMH, ensuring constant concentration polarization. A com­mercial polyamide RO membrane (ESPA1, Nitto, Osaka, Japan) was used. The feed temperature was consistently maintained at 25 ± 1 ◦C using a refrigerated bath circulator (WBL 100, MRC Lab, Holon, Israel). The cross-flow rate was set at 100 L/h, corresponding to a cross-flow velocity of ~0.31 m/s. The permeate was recycled back to the feed tank, and the permeate flux and TMP were recorded by the cross-flow filtration system. Prior to each desalination experiment, the system was washed with recirculating 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solutions for

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: 1 h each, followed by 1 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and 10 mM sodium dodecyl sulfate at pH 11 for 2 h to eliminate inorganic scaling and organic residue. Subsequently, the system was thoroughly washed with DI water. RO membrane compaction was then performed using DI water at 15 bar for approximately 12 h to achieve a stable permeate flux. Finally, real tertiary wastewater effluent (from the Yeruham facility), which had undergone direct coagulation, sand filtration, and chlorina­tion of secondary effluent, and a tertiary effluent, which was produced by filtering comparable secondary effluent with the optimal poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted PES UF membrane, were introduced to the cross-flow system. Desalination performances were compared during filtration for 12 h. 2.7. Water quality and fouling analysis UV absorbance of the water sample at 254 nm (UV254) was measured using a UV spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). A pH meter, conductivity meter, and turbidimeter (2100AN, Hach, Love­land, CO, USA) were used to measure the pH, electrical conductivity (EC), and turbidity of the water samples, respectively. Total suspended solids (TSS) were determined following a standard method using glass fiber filter paper with a pore size of 0.7 μm. The total bacterial amount in the water samples was determined by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS; Attune NxT Flow Cytometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: the water samples was determined by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS; Attune NxT Flow Cytometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Prior to FACS measurement, the cell was fixed and stained sequentially using glutaraldehyde and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid to retain the morphology and increase the fluorescence signal for sub­sequent analysis. The composition of the fouling layer on the membrane surface was analyzed following its extraction by a method based on that developed by Liu and Fang [31]. Half of a fouled membrane coupon (about 7.6 cm2) was cut into small pieces and vortexed with 0.85 % NaCl (7 mL) for 45 min to remove the fouled layer from the membrane surface. Then, 36 % formaldehyde (42 μL) was added to the solution and gently shaken at 4 ◦C for 1 h. Next, 1 M NaOH (2.8 mL) was added to the solution to facilitate dissociation of the fouling layer, and the solution was gently shaken for an additional 3 h at 4 ◦C. The suspension was filtered using a 0.22 μm PVDF filter (Millipore), and dialyzed through a dialysis mem­brane with a MWCO of 500–1000 Da (Spectra/Por, Repligen, Waltham, MA, USA) against DI water at 4 ◦C until the conductivity of the solution

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: Scheme 1. Reaction scheme for grating poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrushes onto the surface of PES UF membranes. H. Huang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124537 dropped below 1 μS/cm. The extracellular protein concentration in the extracted fouling layer was determined by colorimetry using a QPRO bicinchoninic acid kit (Cyanagen Srl, Bologna, Italy). Polysaccharide content was determined by colorimetric assay using a glucose standard [32]. The extracted fouling layer was expressed as dissolved organic carbon (DOC) concentration measured using a total organic carbon analyzer (Multi N/C 3100, Analytik Jena, Jena, Germany) [33]. 3. Results and discussions 3.1. Membrane modification and characterization The concentration of hydroxyl groups was optimized to obtain a sufficient density of anchor points for the ATRP initiator, while main­taining the membrane’s permeability within the range for UF. To ach­ieve this, surface modification with varying concentrations of HEMA monomer (from 0 to 70 mM) via free-radical UV irradiation was explored. The ATR-FTIR results (Fig. S2a) revealed that grafting was achieved at a HEMA concentration as low as 10 mM, as evidenced by a new carbonyl peak at 1730 cm-1. The ATR-FTIR spectra indicated that the grafting degree (the ratio between the intensity of the new carbonyl peak and the membrane peak at 1580 cm-1) increased with increasing HEMA concentrations.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ng degree (the ratio between the intensity of the new carbonyl peak and the membrane peak at 1580 cm-1) increased with increasing HEMA concentrations. However, its reproducibility decreased, as evi­denced by the increasing deviation in the grafting degree. The perme­ability of the PES-OH membrane reduced dramatically at a HEMA concentration of 50 mM (Fig. S2b). Considering grafting homogeneity and membrane permeability, a HEMA concentration of 30 mM was selected for subsequent AGET–SI–ATRP. The poly(SBMA) brush was then grafted by AGET–SI–ATRP from the surface of the PES-OH membrane. The grafting was verified by XPS and ATR-FTIR analyses. The XPS results of the PES and PES-OH membranes in Table 1 confirmed the HEMA grafting, as indicated by a marked in­crease in the O content (supporting the ATR-FTIR results discussed above). The minor increase in S content in the PES-OH membrane is likely due to XPS measurement variability (differences between samples, surface heterogeneity, or depth variations) or from leaching of additives in the PES added by the manufacturer [34] (probably poly­vinylpyrrolidone (PVP), as suggested by the N content), which could increase the relative S fraction. The Br3d signal (0.3 %) in the PES–OH–Br membrane (C–Br bond) confirmed the attachment of the ATRP initiator (BiBB); its disappearance below the detection limit following grafting suggests successful chain initiation and polymer growth.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: of the ATRP initiator (BiBB); its disappearance below the detection limit following grafting suggests successful chain initiation and polymer growth. The grafting of the poly(SBMA) was evident by changes in the high-resolution XPS N1s scan spectra (Fig. S3). A signal at 399.6 eV in pristine and HEMA-grated membranes indicates the presence of O––C–NH groups on the pristine PES membrane, which may be attrib­uted to the PVP additive. The new N1s signal at 402.7 eV in the M2, M4, and M6 membranes indicated the quaternary alkylammonium motif from grafted poly(SBMA), which increased with polymerization time [35,36]. The ATR-FTIR spectra of the final grafted membranes (M2, M4, and M6) showed signals from the new sulfone bonds (1040 cm-1) and a higher carbonyl signal intensity (1730 cm-1) from SBMA (Fig. 1a) [37–39]. The SBMA grafting degree (defined as the ratio of the peak at

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: 1040 cm-1 to that at 1580 cm-1) increased from 0.04 ± 0.004 (for M2) to 0.12 ± 0.015 (for M6) with increasing polymerization time (Fig. 1b). The poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush grafting changed properties, including physicochemical characteristics, separation performance (permeability and selectivity), and surface and cross-sectional morphology. The CAw of the pristine PES membrane was 42◦± 3◦ (Fig. 2a), lower than a typical cast PES membrane due to the addition of hydrophilic additives to the commercial membrane. This was confirmed by the presence of a new characteristic peak at 1680 cm-1 in the ATRFTIR spectrum and by the XPS analysis (as mentioned above). The CAw following poly(HEMA) modification was reduced to 29◦± 1◦. Attachment of the slightly hydrophobic BiBB slightly increased CAw to 31 ± 1◦[40], whereas poly(SBMA) brush grafting reduced it again, to 25◦± 1◦for membrane M6, the most hydrophilic of the final mem­branes. The zeta potential of the pristine membrane was about -75 mV at pH 7; the three final grafted membranes exhibited similar zeta po­tentials between -50 and -45 mV (Fig. 2b). Although charge neutrality is expected for polyzwitterionic-modified surfaces, negative values were observed here owing to the high negative zeta potential of the pristine PES membrane and the thinness of the final grafted layer (as later described) [30]. The water permeability of the pristine 200 kDa PES was approxi­mately 300 LMH/bar.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: the thinness of the final grafted layer (as later described) [30]. The water permeability of the pristine 200 kDa PES was approxi­mately 300 LMH/bar. This decreased after grafting to approximately 100, 81, and 61 LMH/bar for membranes M2, M4, and M6, respectively (Fig. 2c), probably because of reductions in pore size. This is supported by the decrease in MWCO from around 200 kDa for the pristine PES membrane to approximately 100 kDa for membrane M6 (Fig. 2d). Despite reduced flux compared with the pristine PES membrane, the permeance values of the final grafted membranes remained within the UF range (Fig. 2c and d). The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the pristine 200 kDa PES and modified membranes were characterized using SEM, AFM, and FIB-SEM (Fig. 3). The SEM images showed that poly(SBMA) pseudobottlebrush grafting by AGET–SI–ATRP did not alter the intrinsic membrane structure; clear pores remained visible on all membrane surfaces. However, the pores became narrower, and surface porosity appeared to decrease with increasing polymerization time (Fig. 3a1–a4). AFM indicated that the pristine 200 kDa PES membrane had a relatively smooth surface with a roughness (Ra) value of 3.1 ± 1 nm (Fig. 3b1), which slightly decreased to 2.7 ± 0.5 nm for M2 and 1.7 ± 0.2 nm for M6 (Fig. 3b2–b4). In the high-resolution FIB-SEM cross-sectional images in Fig.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: , which slightly decreased to 2.7 ± 0.5 nm for M2 and 1.7 ± 0.2 nm for M6 (Fig. 3b2–b4). In the high-resolution FIB-SEM cross-sectional images in Fig. 3c1–c4, a white dashed line represents the sputtered platinum layer on the PES and marks the boundary between the membrane surface and the grafted layer. For the pristine PES membrane, the platinum layer on the PES overlapped the carbon/platinum film above, indicating no brush layer (Fig. 3c1). For the three modified membranes, the average thick­ness of the poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush layer increased from 11.6 ±2.8 to 15.1 ± 1.9 nm with increasing reaction time. The limited growth with grafting time suggested that the elongation of SBMA chains was mainly orthogonal to the HEMA chains. Future studies will investigate other grafted structures at the molecular scale to provide further insights into their growth and structures. 3.2. Increased fouling resistance of the modified membranes To evaluate the effect of poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush grafting on the antifouling properties of the membranes, preliminary fouling ex­periments tested BSA as a model foulant with an overall water recovery ratio of ~95 %. Fouling studies commonly use BSA, making it a suitable standard for assessing the antifouling characteristics of the modified membranes [41,42]. The relative TMP change with filtration time for the reference 200 kDa PES membrane increased by approximately 60 % after six filtration cycles (Fig.

Membranes M2 and M4 dis­played shallower TMP increases of around 39 % and 20 %, respectively,

The commercial 100 kDa membrane showed a significantly higher increase in TMP (57 %) compared to the M6 membrane. These results demonstrate the efficiency of poly(SBMA)

H. Huang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124537 pseudo-bottlebrush grafting for reducing membrane fouling; as ex­pected, antifouling performance improved as brush coverage and thickness increased. Given the M6 membrane’s excellent antifouling properties against BSA, it was tested by filtration of real secondary wastewater effluent at a 95 % water recovery ratio for 24 h. Table 2 lists the characteristics of the secondary effluent. Fig. 5a presents the changes in relative TMP of the three membranes before and after backwash during 24 filtration cycles (Fig. S4 shows the changes in the absolute TMP values.). In all cases, relative TMP gradually increased over time, indicating the accumulation of fouling on the membrane surfaces. The 200 kDa PES membrane exhibited the steepest increase in relative TMP during each cycle, showing an increase of approximately 2.4 times by the end of the experiment (from 150 to 360 mbar).

In addition, after each backwash (indicated by the shift from empty to solid circles), the TMP decreased, but did not return to the initial value, indicating that some fouling was irreversible. The relative TMP of the 100 kDa PES membrane followed a similar but less steep trend, doubling from 320 to 640 mbar; each backwash also incompletely recovered the relative TMP. This lower

content: Fig. 4. Changes in relative TMP (TMP/TMP0) during filtration of BSA (0.1 g L-1 in 8.5 mM NaCl and 1.5 mM CaCl2) by pristine 100 and 200 kDa PES membranes and poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted PES membranes after polymerization for 2 h (M2), 4 h (M4), and 6 h (M6). Shading represents the standard deviation (n = 2). H. Huang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124537 fouling propensity might have been due to the 100 kDa PES membrane’s higher hydrophilicity (Fig. 3a) and smaller pore size. The M6 membrane showed the lowest relative TMP increase (1.4 times, from 430 to 600 mbar) of the three membranes. Backwashing consistently returned its relative TMP close to the initial value, indicating resistance to irre­versible fouling. The pseudo-bottlebrush grafting reduced the mem­brane permeability (Fig. 2c), i.e., it had a higher initial TMP value compared to the commercial 100 kDa PES membrane (430 mbar vs. 320 mbar, respectively). Nevertheless, this reduction in permeability should be considered in parallel with the substantial improvement in anti­fouling performance. The poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush grafting significantly enhanced the membrane’s fouling resistance, resulting in a lower final TMP, more stable filtration performance, fewer cleaning cycles, and reduced operational maintenance.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ’s fouling resistance, resulting in a lower final TMP, more stable filtration performance, fewer cleaning cycles, and reduced operational maintenance. In wastewater treatment processes, where fouling control and stable performance are critical considerations, such operational stability is more valuable than a higher initial permeability. The type of fouling was assessed by extracting the fouling layer from each membrane after each fouling experiment, and analyzing the pro­teins, polysaccharides, and DOC concentration (Fig. 5b). As expected, the concentrations of each material on the M6 membrane surface were markedly lower than those on the two pristine PES membranes, consistent with the results for relative TMP. Each membrane showed a substantially higher concentration of DOC than proteins or poly­saccharides, indicating that little biofilm was produced during the 24 h fouling experiments. The dominant fouling was from dissolved organic matter in the wastewater, which was neither protein nor polysaccharide. Fig. 5c–e shows representative SEM images of the membranes after 24 h fouling experiments. Elongated and rod-shaped bacteria were scattered across the surfaces of all membranes; however, the 200 kDa PES membrane (Fig. 5c) exhibited substantially more coverage than the 100 kDa PES membrane (Fig. 5d), in agreement with their correspond­ing increases in TMP (Fig. 5a). This difference was probably due to the greater hydrophilicity of the 100 kDa PES membrane.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ment with their correspond­ing increases in TMP (Fig. 5a). This difference was probably due to the greater hydrophilicity of the 100 kDa PES membrane. In contrast, the M6 membrane (Fig. 5e) showed few bacterial deposits, as expected from its superior antifouling properties. The absence of noticeable biofouling (i. e., bacterial aggregation and extracellular polymeric substances) on the membrane surfaces supported our assumption that organic fouling was the main fouling mechanism during the experiment. The fouling experiment was conducted under challenging, realistic conditions, and in the dead-end setup, the feed concentration in the filtration cell can increase during filtration owing to foulant rejection.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: An increase in feed concentration occurred, as indicated by the increase in UV254 absorbance and DOC concentration in the feed, and thus also in the permeate (Fig. S5). The increased concentration of foulants probably caused a slight rise in the TMP of the membranes during filtration. It was assumed that maintaining a constant feed composition, similar to actual membrane processes, would lead to even lower fouling. To test this assumption, fouling experiments were conducted with the feed in the dead-end filtration cell being replaced every 6 h (corresponding to the time required to filter around 1.5 cell volume). Fig. 5f shows that these conditions substantially reduced the increases in relative TMP for the M6 and 100 kDa PES membranes. The increase in relative TMP for the 100 kDa membrane was reduced by half, but was still around 1.45 times, showing that there was still irreversible fouling. In contrast, the M6 membrane exhibited superior antifouling properties, with only about a 10 % TMP increase (from 522 to 575 mbar) after operation for 24 h. In addition, backwashing at the end of the filtration cycle almost fully restored the relative TMP for the M6 membrane with no chemical cleaning, indicating its stable antifouling performance.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: filtration cycle almost fully restored the relative TMP for the M6 membrane with no chemical cleaning, indicating its stable antifouling performance. To further vali­date the long-term antifouling performance of the poly(SBMA) pseudobottlebrush-grafted membrane, we conducted an extended 5-day filtration test at constant permeate flux of 18 LMH using secondary effluent with the feed replaced in the filtration cell every 12 h. As shown in Fig. S6, the M6 membrane maintained a stable TMP profile throughout the 120 h test, with TMP/TMP0 increasing only 1.24-fold. In contrast, the commercial 100 kDa PES membrane experienced a sharp relative TMP increase exceeding 2.36-fold. These results confirm the superior fouling resistance and operational stability of the poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush-grafted membrane, further highlighting its potential for long-term use in real-world wastewater treatment applications. This finding is consistent with the long-term stability of a polyamide RO membrane grafted with similar brush architecture during a 7-day brackish water desalination experiment reported in our previous study [28]. Effluent quality is another important consideration for UF mem­branes for pretreatment. Table 2 lists the water quality parameters of the secondary wastewater effluent and UF permeates from the three mem­branes. Generally, UF pretreatment greatly reduced the turbidity, TSS, and total bacteria.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ary wastewater effluent and UF permeates from the three mem­branes. Generally, UF pretreatment greatly reduced the turbidity, TSS, and total bacteria. The reductions of DOC, polysaccharide, and protein concentrations and UV254 absorbance following UF pretreatment indi­cate that a large fraction of the organic compounds that could foul a RO membrane during a subsequent desalination step were rejected. The 100 kDa PES and M6 membranes showed comparable permeate qualities (better than that from the 200 kDa PES membrane) owing to their similar MWCOs. To highlight the poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush layer’s excellent resistance to fouling by organic matter in the secondary effluent, we used QCM-D measurements to evaluate foulant–surface interactions (Fig. 6a). Gold and PES membrane-mimetic sensors were also measured. The poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush mimetic sensor (M6 membrane mimetic sensor) was prepared by grafting the pseudo-bottlebrush structure from the surface of a PES membrane mimetic sensor using the same protocol used to make membrane M6 (Text S1). The PES coating and subsequent grafting of the poly(SBMA) brush layer were confirmed by CAw, Variable angle ATR-FTIR, and XPS analyses (Fig. S7and Table S1). There was an immediate and rapid decrease in frequency for the gold and PES membrane mimetic sensors upon EfOM injection, with shifts of approximately -17 and -13 Hz, respectively (Fig. 6b), indicating strong foulant adsorption.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: mbrane mimetic sensors upon EfOM injection, with shifts of approximately -17 and -13 Hz, respectively (Fig. 6b), indicating strong foulant adsorption. Conversely, the M6 membrane mimetic sensor showed negligible frequency change, suggesting mini­mal interaction with EfOM. Furthermore, both the gold and PES mem­brane mimetic sensors displayed pronounced dissipation shifts, indicating the formation of a thick, hydrated, and viscoelastic foulant layer (Fig. 6c). In contrast, the M6 membrane mimetic sensor exhibited only a slight dissipation shift, confirming the absence of major foulant adhesion and the maintenance of a rigid, non-fouling surface [43]. These results confirmed the excellent antifouling properties of poly(SBMA)

The superior antifouling properties of the poly(SBMA) pseudobottlebrush layer likely arose from its increased hydrophilicity and steric hindrance [44]. Increased hydrophilicity promoted the formation of a stable hydration layer, which prevented foulants, such as organic compounds and proteins, from adhering to the membrane surface [45]. The poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush layer also acted as a physical bar­rier, obstructing the direct contact of large foulant molecules or particles with the membrane surface [46]. These factors decreased the fouling propensity of the membrane surface substantially.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: 3.3. Improved RO desalination of UF pretreated effluent using the grafted membrane The RO desalination performance of secondary wastewater pre­treated using the M6 membrane (UF pretreatment) was compared with that of real tertiary wastewater effluent conventionally treated by coagulation, sand filtration, and chlorination. Table S2 presents the quality of each effluent feed. The main difference was that the UFpretreated effluent showed significantly lower TSS (e.g., 0 mg/L vs. 2.5 mg/L), reduced turbidity (0.11 NTU vs. 1.1 NTU), and a marked decrease in UV254 absorbance (0.115 cm-1 vs. 0.158 cm-1) and total bacterial amount (25 cell/μL vs. 160 cell/μL). These improvements in quality were expected to reduce the fouling of the RO membrane and improve its desalination performance. Desalination of the convention­ally treated effluent resulted in a steady increase in TMP that started at about 6.2 bar and reached approximately 7 bar after 12 h. In contrast, TMP increased slightly from 6 to 6.2 bar when using UF-pretreated effluent, demonstrating that UF pretreatment showed superior effi­ciency to conventional tertiary treatment in improving RO desalination performance (Fig. 7a). In addition, RO desalination experiment using tertiary effluent pretreated by the commercial 100 kDa PES membrane, which has a similar MWCO to the M6 membrane, was also conducted. As shown in Fig.

This is comparable to the TMP behavior observed for the M6

Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124537 membrane-pretreated effluent, as expected due to the similar water quality produced by the two membranes during pretreatment (Table S2). This result confirms that fouling of the RO membrane is low when the feed is pretreated with membranes with similar separation characteristics, reinforcing the effectiveness of the low-fouling M6 membrane as a pretreatment option for RO effluent desalination. The fouled RO membranes were analyzed using SEM and ATR-FTIR to elucidate the fouling behavior of the two feeds.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: option for RO effluent desalination. The fouled RO membranes were analyzed using SEM and ATR-FTIR to elucidate the fouling behavior of the two feeds. SEM images of the RO membranes at various locations (inlet (1), center (2), and outlet (3)) after their use to desalinate the conventionally treated (Fig. 7c1–3) and UF-pretreated (Fig. 7d1–3) effluents. The conventional tertiary effluent led to much more severe fouling than that prepared by UF and covered the center of the RO membrane with a thick fouling layer, and the polyamide membrane’s characteristic “hills and valleys” structure dis­appeared (Fig. 7c2). In contrast, the intrinsic surface structure of the RO membrane remained visible in most areas after desalination of the UFpretreated effluent (Fig. 7d2). Surprisingly, a small number of micro­organisms were also observed (highlighted in red circles). Microorgan­isms on the membrane surface probably came from contamination during effluent collection or regrowth occurring in the effluent after collection. The nature of the fouling was investigated by comparing ATR-FTIR spectra obtained from the center of each fouled RO membrane with that obtained from a pristine RO membrane (Fig. 7b). The spectrum of the RO membrane that desalinated UF-pretreated effluent closely resembled that of the pristine RO membrane, with only a small increase in the peak at 3200–3450 cm-1, attributed to hydrogen bonding [47].

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: ent closely resembled that of the pristine RO membrane, with only a small increase in the peak at 3200–3450 cm-1, attributed to hydrogen bonding [47]. In contrast, the spectrum of the membrane that desalinated real tertiary effluent exhibited large increases in the peaks at 3200–3450, 1500–1700, and 1000–1100 cm-1, along with a new peak between 2850 and 3080 cm-1. These peaks corresponded to hydrogen bonds, amide groups, C–O groups, and phenoxy groups, respectively, suggesting the presence of proteins and polysaccharides (or carbohydrates) [33,48]. In addition, the reduced signal from the RO membrane suggested the for­mation of a thick fouling layer. 4. Conclusion This study highlights the remarkable performance of a poly(SBMA) pseudo-bottlebrush layer developed by AGET–SI–ATRP grafting in mitigating membrane fouling during the filtration of secondary waste­water effluent at a high water recovery ratio. The grafting enhanced membrane surface hydrophilicity and reduced surface charge and roughness. The growth of brush layer thickness with grafting time confirmed the unique pseudo-bottlebrush structure. The grafting significantly reduced foulant adsorption and bacterial deposition during the filtration of real secondary wastewater effluent, leading to only a 24 % relative increase in TMP over 5 days at a 95 % water recovery ratio, highlighting its excellent antifouling properties.

citation.title: 3D zwitterionic brush-grafted ultrafiltration membranes for fouling-resistant effluent pretreatment and improved reverse osmosis desalination

content: fluent, leading to only a 24 % relative increase in TMP over 5 days at a 95 % water recovery ratio, highlighting its excellent antifouling properties. The QCM-D analysis supported the convention that the excellent antifouling properties of the 3D brush are due to increased hydrophilicity and steric hindrance, which resulted in reduced organic matter adsorption. Moreover, the high-quality effluent resulting from UF using this grafted membrane greatly improved the performance of subsequent RO effluent desalina­tion, compared with using conventional tertiary-treated effluent. Overall, the unique pseudo-bottlebrush grafting strategy developed here also has great potential for further improvements by brush struc­tural optimization. This versatile modification method can be adapted to various membrane types and customized for a wide range of water
